critique.summary: The article describes Flash-MoE, a system that runs a 397 billion parameter Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model on a MacBook Pro with 48GB RAM using a custom C/Metal inference engine. It details the technical innovations enabling efficient model streaming from SSD and GPU acceleration to achieve production-quality output on limited hardware.
mainPoints: Flash-MoE runs the Qwen3.5-397B-A17B model with 397 billion parameters on a MacBook Pro with 48GB RAM.
The model weights (209GB) are streamed from SSD on demand using a custom Metal compute pipeline without Python or frameworks.
Key optimizations include an FMA-optimized dequant kernel, hand-written Metal compute shaders, deferred GPU expert compute, and trusting the OS page cache for expert weight caching.
The system achieves about 4.4 tokens/second with 4-bit quantization and supports tool calling with production-quality output.
The architecture uses 60 transformer layers with 512 experts per layer, activating 4 experts per token plus one shared expert.
The project includes extensive experiments, optimizations, and a detailed pipeline design to maximize performance on Apple Silicon hardware.
critique.truthfulness: The claims in the article are consistent with known techniques in efficient large model inference, such as Mixture-of-Experts architectures, quantization, and GPU acceleration. The repository is publicly available on GitHub with detailed technical documentation and code, supporting the feasibility of running very large models on consumer hardware using streaming and optimized kernels. The performance metrics and hardware specifications align with Apple's M3 Max capabilities. However, independent benchmarks or third-party validations are not cited, so while the technical details appear credible, external confirmation would strengthen the claims.
considerations: The article does not mention potential limitations such as latency variability due to SSD streaming or how well the system scales with different hardware configurations.
There is no discussion of the model's accuracy or quality compared to other implementations or larger hardware setups.
The impact of using 2-bit quantization on output quality and tool calling reliability is noted but not deeply analyzed.
The repository appears to be a research or experimental project rather than a production-ready system, which may affect usability and stability.
The article does not address energy consumption or thermal constraints when running such a large model on a laptop.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the feasibility and practicality of running large language models like Qwen 3.5 397B on consumer hardware using techniques such as 2-bit quantization and expert reduction. While some praise the technical achievement and potential for offline inference, others criticize the significant quality degradation and slow performance, questioning the real-world usability of such approaches.

comments.author: tarruda
comments.text:
Note that this is not the only way to run Qwen 3.5 397B on consumer devices, there are excellent ~2.5 BPW quants available that make it viable for 128G devices.

I've had great success (~20 t/s) running it on a M1 Ultra with room for 256k context. Here are some lm-evaluation-harness results I ran against it:

    mmlu: 87.86%

    gpqa diamond: 82.32%

    gsm8k: 86.43%

    ifeval: 75.90%
More details of my experience:

- https://huggingface.co/ubergarm/Qwen3.5-397B-A17B-GGUF/discu...

- https://huggingface.co/ubergarm/Qwen3.5-397B-A17B-GGUF/discu...

- https://gist.github.com/simonw/67c754bbc0bc609a6caedee16fef8...

Overall an excellent model to have for offline inference.
comments.analysis: Provides concrete performance metrics and references to additional resources, offering a balanced and informed perspective on running large models on consumer hardware.

comments.author: Aurornis
comments.text:
Reading the details, he is using 2-bit quantization and reduced the number of experts per token from 10 down to 4 to get 5 tokens/sec. Cool proof of concept but it’s far from the quality and performance of the 397B model as normally used. Dropping the number of experts is particularly misleading.

This is some interesting work, but applying such extreme measures to LLMs to get them to run severely degrades quality. I know he claims negligible quality loss, but in my experience 2-bit quantizations are completely useless for real work. You can get them to respond to prompts, but they lose their intelligence and will go around in circles.

He also shows 5-6 tokens per second. Again that’s impressive for a large model on limited hardware but it’s very slow. Between the severely degraded model abilities and the extremely slow output the 397B result should be considered an attempt at proving something can technically run, not evidence that it can run well and produce output you’d expect from a 397B model.

He even mentions the obvious problems with his changes:

> *2-bit quantization produces \name\ instead of "name" in JSON output, making tool calling unreliable.

So right out of the gate this isn’t useful if you want to do anything with it. He could have tried smaller models or less quantizations to get actual useful output from the model, but it wouldn’t look as impressive. It’s honestly getting kind of exhausting to read all of these AI-coded (admitted in the link) and AI-written papers made more for resume building. It would have been interesting to see this work applied to running a useful model that hadn’t been lobotomized instead of applying tricks to get an impressive headline but useless output.
comments.analysis: Critically evaluates the trade-offs of extreme quantization and expert reduction, highlighting the impact on model quality and speed, and cautioning against overhyping such results.

comments.author: justacatbot
comments.text: The quality degradation at 2-bit is a real issue. For actual work tasks, a well-tuned 30B at 4-bit usually outperforms a 70B+ at 2-bit in my experience. The expert reduction on top of that compounds things - you're essentially running a fairly different model. Still interesting to see the upper bound of what consumer hardware can attempt, even if the result isn't production-ready.
comments.analysis: Supports the critique on quality degradation with practical experience, emphasizing that smaller, better-quantized models often outperform larger, heavily quantized ones.

comments.author: mkw
comments.text:
TLDR I took a stab at leveraging Dan's work and making it more practical:

https://github.com/matt-k-wong/mlx-flash

2 bit quantization lobotomizes the model but is impressive nonetheless! Maybe one day we'll be able to have intelligent 2 bit quants... I wonder.

my version supports - 4bit quantization, hybrid streaming (Disk + ram), arbitrary model compatibility, tested on Mamba2, and lets up the framework for LM Studio integration

I leveraged this work (Credit to Danveloper) and am in the middle of making this work on more practical models and quants. It still uses flash streaming, but done so with a control knob so you can choose how much ram and how little ram to use. In the craziest case, it uses as little ram as possible but is very slow, however, in the balanced case you use some ram and it's much faster.

I designed it around the intelligence dense Nemotron 3 Nano 30B and Nemotron Cascade 2 30B models (which are smaller, more intelligence density) and can run on low end 16GB machines, though you can run arbitrarily large models on larger machines (designed for very low end, but capable of high end).
comments.analysis: Shares a practical extension of the original work, acknowledging limitations of 2-bit quantization while proposing improvements and supporting more usable models on lower-end hardware.

comments.author: rvz
comments.text:
The technical write up is great, but Mac users should not get too excited just yet on running 300B+ parameter models locally as the TPS isn't that good.

>...at 4.4+ tokens/second

That is even when it is using 4-bit quantization and it is still at that speed.

> The entire 209GB model streams from SSD through a custom Metal compute pipeline.

This is my main problem.

If I were to run this on a Mac SSD, 24/7 for heavy usage such as Openclaw, that is going to significantly reduce the lifetime of the SSD.

Can't imagine using this in the long term right now, but improvements will follow. Still a great write up anyways.
comments.analysis: Offers a realistic perspective on performance limitations and hardware wear concerns, tempering enthusiasm with practical considerations about long-term usability.

comments.author: zozbot234
comments.text: The github page mentions that a naïve mmap approach is bottlenecked by per-page overhead. Can this be mitigated by setting up explicit "huge" pages? (2M using the CONT PTE feature if the "native" page size is 16k; 32M using a PMD level block mapping; or 1G using the CONT PMD feature.) Does macOS support this out of the box?  Alternatively, one might use a simple mmap and then something like posix_fadvise to set up prefetching of the data.
comments.analysis: Poses a technical question about memory management optimizations that could improve performance, showing engagement with system-level details.

comments.author: JSR_FDED
comments.text: This is a very impressive result. If I understand correctly the bottleneck is the SSD in this architecture - the author seems to get almost 15GB/s - but I seem to remember the max b/w was about 8GB/s. What am I missing?
comments.analysis: Raises a valid question about hardware bandwidth limits, indicating critical engagement with the reported performance figures.

comments.author: bertili
comments.text: Very impressive! I wonder if there is a similar path for Linux using system memory instead of SSD? Hell, maybe even a case for the return of some kind of ROMs of weights?
comments.analysis: Speculates on alternative hardware configurations and future directions, contributing constructively to the discussion.

comments.author: haomingkoo
comments.text: Really interesting approach. Curious how the 2-bit quantization affects the model's reasoning ability on longer chains of thought vs shorter prompts. The benchmarkslook solid but real-world usage seems like a different story based on the comments here.
comments.analysis: Expresses curiosity about the practical implications of quantization on reasoning, reflecting a nuanced understanding of model evaluation.

comments.author: 383toast
comments.analysis: Briefly expresses frustration with the slow token generation speed, a common concern in the discussion.
